ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Ayam Masak Bali; mapped to broader 'chicken dish' class (FOODON_03301521) as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Ayam Masak Bali; A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) used as the closest defensible broader anchor for this braised/sautéed chicken dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Malay chicken preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Ayam Masak Bali is approximately 250 g, representing one portion of chicken pieces with sauce, commonly served alongside white rice or tomato rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritional Composition of Malay Chicken Dishes — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for Malay chicken gravy dishes. Ayam Masak Bali is not listed as a standalone entry; values are derived from the closest HPB-documented Malay braised/sautéed chicken dish with sweet soy sauce seasoning. Micronutrient values for minerals and vitamins are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken and spice paste components where HPB data is not granular. Saturated fat estimated at 2.8 g/100 g based on HPB chicken curry fat profile proportions.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; analogous estimate for mixed chicken dish with sweet soy sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Masak Bali. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy to mixed braised chicken dishes with sweet soy sauce components documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care. The protein and fat content of the chicken significantly attenuates postprandial glucose response relative to the sugar contributed by kicap manis. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 20.5) / 100 ≈ 9.2, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 8.2 × 2.5 = 20.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from kicap manis (sweet soy sauce) and added salt; 310 mg sodium per 100 g equates to approximately 775 mg per standard 250 g serving.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g and request reduced soy sauce preparation, as a full 250 g serving provides approximately 775 mg sodium — over 30% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (6.5 g/100 g) from kicap manis partially offset by protein (12.5 g/100 g) and fat (9.5 g/100 g), yielding a low estimated GI of 45 and a moderate glycemic load of approximately 9.2 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Ayam Masak Bali in a standard 250 g serving alongside a controlled portion of low-GI rice, monitoring total carbohydrate intake to remain within the 45–60 g per meal target recommended by diabetes dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (9.5 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat of 2.8 g/100 g from cooking oil and chicken skin; high sodium further elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken, use minimal cooking oil, and limit sweet soy sauce to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake, as a 250 g serving contributes approximately 7 g saturated fat and 775 mg sodium toward daily cardiovascular risk thresholds.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice components (ginger, lemongrass, galangal) may provide modest benefit, though processed soy sauce and added sugar may be mildly pro-inflammatory for some individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume this dish in moderation, favouring preparations with reduced sweet soy sauce and emphasising the anti-inflammatory spice paste components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Masak Bali is a traditional Malay chicken dish widely prepared in Malaysian home kitchens and commonly served at lunch and dinner. Despite its name referencing Bali, the dish is firmly rooted in Malay culinary tradition rather than Balinese cuisine. The preparation involves first deep-frying or pan-frying chicken pieces until golden, then simmering them in a richly aromatic spice paste made from pounded lemongrass, galangal, ginger, shallots, garlic, and dried chilies. A defining characteristic is the generous use of lemongrass, which is both blended into the paste and bruised whole to maximise fragrance. The dish is seasoned with kicap manis (sweet soy sauce), which imparts a deep caramelised sweetness, a glossy dark sauce, and a distinctive umami depth. The result is a thick, intensely flavoured sauce that coats the chicken pieces. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 165 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 9.5 g fat, and 8.2 g carbohydrate, with 310 mg sodium and 6.5 g sugar primarily from the sweet soy sauce. The estimated glycemic index is 45 (low), moderated by the protein and fat content. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium and requires portion control for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is suitable in moderation for Type 2 Diabetes when paired with a controlled carbohydrate accompaniment. The spice paste ingredients — ginger, galangal, and lemongrass — contribute bioactive anti-inflammatory compounds. The dish is halal and dairy-free but contains soy allergens from kicap manis.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for Malay braised chicken dishes; Ayam Masak Bali is not listed as a standalone entry in any approved primary source. Values are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 2.8 g/100 g based on HPB Malay chicken dish fat profile proportions; no direct measurement available for this specific preparation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Ayam Masak Bali are not available as a standalone entry in HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Ayam Masak Bali exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (45 × 20.5) / 100 ≈ 9.2. 4. Vitamin A reported as mcg RAE estimated from beta-carotene contribution of spice paste ingredients. 5. Folate reported as mcg DFE. 6. The dish is not from Bali, Indonesia; the name is a Malay culinary convention. 7. Soy allergen present from kicap manis. 8. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are broader-class matches; no exact ontology code exists for this dish.
